input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A laboratory‑scale gantry crane system, comprising the mechanical gantry frame, rails, hoist assembly, trolley and load attachment, together with an embedded controller that commands and receives sensor feedback. The crane operates in a controlled laboratory environment for experimental and educational purposes, providing a well‑defined kinematic subsystem that can be modeled with CAD tools. Its small scale, readily measurable degrees of freedom and open mechanical design make it a suitable candidate for digital twin development, enabling high‑fidelity validation against a physically realisable reference.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers real‑time monitoring of the crane’s state via telemetry from sensors on the physical system, optimal control computation that derives feed‑forward commands to minimize deviation from desired trajectories, historical data logging of all sensor and actuator streams for post‑hoc analysis, continuous validation that adjudicates the fidelity of the kinematic CAD model by comparing predicted versus measured motion, and data visualisation dashboards that render the current and past operations. Users include researchers conducting validation, fault‑detection studies and optimization experiments, and educators demonstrating cyber‑physical integration.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin implementation relies exclusively on freely available and commonly used open‑source software. The physical side is interfaced through a standard controller, while the digital side is constructed using CAD modelling of the crane geometry, a kinematic model encoded in an open format, data logging via a local database, and visualization tools that render the kinematic model and measured data in real time. No proprietary commercial software or custom hardware is required.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The GreenhouseDT explorer orchestrates its digital twin through a micro‑service style architecture that relies heavily on containerization or virtualization for rapid deployment. Sensor data streams from a Raspberry‑Pi based physical greenhouse are published to an Apache ActiveMQ message broker, which decouples the ingestion layer from downstream analytics and control services. The twin’s state is persisted in an InfluxDB time‑series database, accessible via its HTTP API or Telegraf agents. Virtual machine images bundle prerecorded data for replay and testing scenarios, while the entire stack (including the message broker, database, and twin logic) can be assembled from Docker containers for reproducibility. For the Air Quality Management exemplar, the twin leverages an AutomationML model to describe asset relationships and uses Microsoft Azure services (e.g., Azure IoT Hub, Azure Functions, Azure Sentinel) to implement runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction pipelines. Overall, the system coordinates resources via message‑driven event buses, uses cloud or on‑premise deployment patterns, and manages compute and storage through VM image snapshots and Docker images.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through standardized middleware and cloud connectors. Physical subsystems (e.g., Raspberry‑Pi controllers) push telemetry to the twin via ActiveMQ JMS queues, while the twin exposes its state through InfluxDB’s RESTful line‑protocol endpoint for downstream analytics. The Air Quality twin utilizes AutomationML to exchange asset metadata and configuration with enterprise PLM systems or other twins. Azure services—such as IoT Hub for device connectivity, Azure Functions for serverless orchestration, and Azure Sentinel for security analytics—provide managed integration points that connect the twin to broader enterprise IT fabrics. All components are exposed over standard protocols (HTTP, WebSocket for IoT Hub, JMS for ActiveMQ) ensuring interoperability with legacy or third‑party systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: GreenhouseDT employs a self‑adaptive digital twin architecture that models the low‑cost greenhouse system. The twin’s behavioral adaptation logic runs inside virtual machine images that pre‑seed an InfluxDB time‑series database; inter‑module communication is handled by an ActiveMQ message broker. The Air Quality Management exemplar uses an AutomationML‑based digital twin to represent the indoor air quality control system, and data originates from a Raspberry‑Pi board equipped with a generic sensor suite. In both exemplars, data streams from the physical sensors are ingested into Azure services as part of a runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction pipeline, providing a continuous data‑to‑model loop that supports validation against the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Logging Application continuously collects crane state data (e.g., position, velocity, load) through the Physical Crane Interface and writes this information to a TimescaleDB database via local network communication. Data is stored as database rows; the document does not specify the exact data types, transmission frequency, or any preprocessing or quality‑assurance steps.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are issued by the Control Application, which uses the Physical Crane Interface to send movement or trajectory instructions to the crane hardware via a Python program. The command format, authentication, and feedback mechanisms are not detailed; execution confirmation is inferred indirectly through the state logs captured by the Logging Application.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Components communicate via an MQTT broker that facilitates signaling between services such as the Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service, and Continuous Validation Service. The architecture follows a container-based model; specific bandwidth, latency, network topology, or security protocol details are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All components run on local infrastructure—a lab environment—using Python applications, a TimescaleDB database, and a Grafana dashboard. Deployment is on‑premise; scalability, cloud support, redundancy, and availability mechanisms are not described.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management exemplar offers runtime visualization of indoor sensor streams, a simulation engine for air flow and contaminant dispersion, and a prediction module calculating future air quality indices to aid virus‑spread prevention. Data from a Raspberry‑Pi sensor array is transmitted via MQTT through ActiveMQ into an InfluxDB time‑series store; Azure Machine Learning pipelines ingest this data, generate forecasts, and render them in a dashboard that informs on‑site decision makers and can trigger automated ventilation controls.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin model conforms to the AutomationML specification, ensuring machine‑equipment data interoperability with CAPP and CAM systems. Communication between physical and virtual layers uses MQTT, an industry‑wide open‑standard messaging protocol supported by ActiveMQ. Data persistence employs InfluxDB, an open‑source time‑series database that follows the OpenTSDB schema. Version control and collaboration are handled via Git on GitHub, following best practices for open‑source software development.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)





















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.
